data.title: The Blockade: Stealth De-Escalation Is Working
data.description:
We've seen an epic rally over the last two weeks or so. In fact, the move has been so parabolic that it's something I have not witnessed since perhaps the Fed-juiced Coronavirus surge. Now, despite the enormous optimism in markets, there are still near-term uncertainties and the potential for a consolidation or a pullback phase.

Why There Could Be A Pullback/Consolidation Phase

Strictly from a technical perspective, stocks have rallied so much that many names and even major market averages have become considerably overbought here.

Just look at the S&P 500 "SPX" (SP500):

S&P 500 1-Year Chart

Two weeks ago "the sky was falling," yet now we're at new all-time highs "ATHs." The SPX has rallied from a low of roughly 6,340 to a recent high close to 7,080 (SPX futures), a highly impressive rally of roughly 12% in only about two weeks.

Now, this is not a typical rally. Nonetheless, that does not mean that the market won't go through a pullback/consolidation phase soon. The RSI has gone from below 30 roughly two weeks ago to around 70; an unprecedented reversal in sentiment and momentum in such a short time frame.

Uncertainties Still Persist

I may have overreacted slightly to the blockade, selling and decreasing several of my portfolio positions before the major run-ups over the last few days. However, after a reassessment of the blockade strategy.

I realized that while the move is escalatory in nature, it should actually lead to de-escalation in the general conflict. The Iranian economy is getting choked off without a reasonable response to the U.S.'s blockade strategy. Therefore, it may be a brilliant move on the side of the U.S., and while it may be risky, so far, it's going well.

Now, as Iran struggles for oil revenues, it may be more open to coming to the table and making a more reasonable deal with the U.S. over its uranium enrichment program, and other crucial sticking points. In short, Iran may have literally run out of cards, as its ultimate leverage strategy of extorting the Strait of Hormuz has been flipped on its head.

It was an unexpected and an extremely strong show of force, without having to blast power stations and blow up bridges, which would have made the situation much worse. Which is partly why the market has rallied so hard here recently.

Still, despite the parabolic price action in equities, uncertainties regarding the timing and the exact details of the Iran deal remain unclear. It does appear like the market is pricing in an ultra-bullish "base-case" scenario, and it's not a fact that things will go exactly to plan.

Additionally, there are earnings. The big banks have reported, with most providing better than anticipated EPS and revenue results. Still, there are some concerns regarding guidance and the potential for an increase in credit card, and potentially other defaults.

Also, there is the potential for increased inflation for longer, especially if a concrete agreement regarding the Strait is not achieved soon. The higher energy costs may also be weighing more on consumer sentiment and spending, as was shown in some recent numbers.

Consumer Sentiment Drops Like A Rock

Consumer sentiment actually hit its worst reading on record, a 47.6 level, well below the 51.6 consensus estimate. The current average (around a record high) 25% credit card rates in the U.S. are not helping consumers, many of whom remain heavily indebted in all sorts of debt (credit card, student loan, mortgages, auto loans, etc.).

Also, an interesting factor is that we do not typically see such negative consumer sentiment during healthy market cycles. Instead, we've seen lows of approximately 50 or so around recession bottoms, like in the early 1980s, 2009, 2011, and 2022, but why now?

It's possible that the consumer is in worse shape than the market perceives. It also appears like we are going through a potential decoupling of Wall Street and Main Street. Stocks are at ATHs again while the Main Street economy may be struggling, and the issues could worsen going forward.

Still No Clarity On The Fed

The market enjoys clarity, especially if it concerns monetary policy. Unfortunately, we are quite a bit away from a clear monetary perspective. When will the Fed cut interest rates? By how much? Could the Fed actually tighten rates before cutting?

It seems like almost anything is possible in this highly uncertain and still very volatile market environment. The market currently expects no more cuts this year. There is about a 2/3 probability that the Fed will hold the benchmark at its current rate going into next year. Subsequently, there is only about a 33% probability for one or maybe two rate cuts.

In general, the market appears relatively pessimistic on rate cuts, and I would prefer to see a shift toward a more dovish stance. However, this may require some time, a resolution to the Iran conflict, and a sustainable downtick in oil and other energy-related prices as we advance.

The Bottom Line

The market has had one of its most fierce short-term rallies I've seen in my 25 years of investing. Also, while earnings season got off to a mixed start, there is not a guarantee that tech earnings and guidance will be as stellar as the market anticipates.

Many companies could outperform, but I am becoming more concerned about the continued growth in the AI buildout process, as it could show more considerable signs of slowing as we advance. I am also concerned about the circular financing and whether companies like OpenAI and others will be able to meet their massive spending obligations in future years, especially if the credit markets tighten up.

In my opinion, no, they likely will not. This is not only concerning OpenAI, but also Anthropic, CoreWeave (CRWV), Nvidia (NVDA), and many other AI majors and supporting companies, which may inevitably fall victim to the eventual and likely inevitable unwind of the AI bubble. However, this may not occur in the near term, and for now, the AI segment may still have notable growth ahead.

Considering this scenario, I remain intermediate-term bullish while keeping a cautious outlook in the near term. Longer-term, there will likely be substantial issues, and a broad market downturn, bear market, and even a crash scenario could occur (possibly 2-5 years down the line). Provided that the Iran conflict may get resolved soon, the market has considerable constructive tailwinds. Thus, it is likely that we could still have a solid Q2 and send half to 2026.

While I recently decreased my SPX year-end target to a range of 7,300-7,500 on the blockade news (potential overreaction), I did not see the big picture entirely right away. Therefore, I am readjusting to a slightly higher year-end SPX PT range of 7,500-7,700, with a more cautious outlook for future years.

data.title: The Outlook For S&P 500 Dividends In April 2026
data.description:
How has the expected future changed for the quarterly dividends of the S&P 500 (SPX) in the month since our previous snapshot?

As of 15 April 2026, the outlook for the S&P 500's quarterly dividends through the end of 2026 and the first quarter of 2027 has improved considerably over the past month. Here is our summary of how the outlook for the S&P 500's quarterly dividends per share changed since our 13 March 2026 snapshot:

  • 2026-Q2: Increase of $0.21 to $19.77 per share
  • 2026-Q3: Increase of $0.25 to $20.48 per share
  • 2026-Q4: Increase of $0.27 to $20.38 per share
  • 2027-Q1: Increase of $0.55 to $21.74 per share

Not all the dividend futures data was rosy. On the day the dividend futures contract for 2026-Q1 ended on Friday, 20 March 2026, the expected dividend payout for 2026-Q1 plunged by $0.26 per share to a final value of $21.20 per share for the now-ended quarter.

The following chart shows how expectations for the S&P 500's quarterly dividends per share changed in the month from 13 March 2026 to 15 April 2026.

We're going to a rolling 13-quarter presentation for this chart, which, for this edition, covers 2024-Q2 through 2027-Q1 and gives us a first look at the dividend expectations for 2027-Q2. The first projection of that most distant future quarter of this outlook is $20.06 per share, which is $0.29 higher than the $19.77 per share expected for the current quarter of 2026-Q2.

If this is your first exposure to the S&P 500's quarterly dividend futures, be sure to read the following section that explains what this data is communicating about the future for the index's dividends.

More About Dividend Futures Data

For this series, we take a snapshot of the CME Group's S&P 500 quarterly dividend futures data shortly after the second or third week of each month.

Dividend futures indicate the amount of dividends per share to be paid out over the period covered by each quarter's dividend futures contracts, which start on the day after the preceding quarter's dividend futures contracts expire and end on the third Friday of the month ending the indicated quarter. For example, as determined by dividend futures contracts, the now "current" quarter of 2026-Q2 began on Saturday, 21 March 2026, and will end on Friday, 19 June 2026. Since the expectations for this quarter's dividend payouts can change all the way up to that final date, it qualifies as a future quarter.

Because dividend futures are tied to options contracts that run on this schedule, that makes these figures different from the quarterly dividends per share figures that are reported by Standard & Poor's. S&P reports the amount of dividends per share paid out during regular calendar quarters after the end of each quarter. This term mismatch accounts for the differences in dividends reported by both sources, with the biggest differences between the two typically seen in the first and fourth quarters of each year.

Dividend futures data is important for more than just what they project will be a future quarter's dividend payout. They represent the quantified expectations investors have for the future income they will realize from holding their investments, which affects how investors set current-day stock prices. How changes in the outlook for dividends at specific points of time in the future contribute to changes in current-day stock prices is described by this math.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: RENK Group: A Pure Play On Europe's Defense Boom
data.description:
RENK Group (RNKGF) stock has been mostly flat since my last report, contrasting with my buy rating and view that RENK Group could be among the beneficiaries of Europe's push for security autonomy. The stock already lost steam in 2025 after the capital markets day in which the company showed that there would be little revenue growth acceleration in the near term. The war in Iran has not strengthened sentiment, as investors are increasingly growing aware that near-term demand is robust and the war only adds input cost concerns. In this report, I discuss RENK Group's FY2025 results and update my price target in support of my buy rating.

Strong Growth For RENK Group In 2025

In 2025, RENK Group saw its revenues increase 20% to €1.37 billion, with defense sales growing from 72% of revenues to 74% of revenues. New build sales now account for 64% of revenues compared to 60% a year ago, with the remainder attributable to aftermarket sales. Adjusted EBIT grew 22% to €230 million, with margins increasing 30 basis points to 16.9%. We see that margin impact is modest, but if we consider that the company has to invest to grow the business and ramp up production and is seeing sales shift somewhat from higher-margin aftermarket sales to lower-margin new build sales, I do believe that a 30 basis point expansion is actually quite good. Order intake grew 9% to €1.6 billion with a book-to-bill ratio of 1.15x, which shows that there is not a lot of near-term opportunity to accelerate revenue growth, or at least it is not captured in the backlog yet.

Defense revenues grew 24% to a little over €1 billion, with order intake growing 4% to €1.17 billion. We note that some expected program awards for navy programs and main battle tank programs shifted into 2026. If those had occurred in 2025, the order intake would have been €1.37 billion.

Vehicle Mobility, which includes tracked and heavy military vehicles, grew revenues by 24.8% to €872 million, with EBIT increasing 27.8% to €178 million. Margins expanded 40 basis points to 20.4%. Order intake grew 11% to €1.13 billion, indicating a 1.3x book-to-bill ratio. Marine & Industrial sales grew 15.3% to €580 million with 30% EBIT growth to €45 million and margins growing strongly from 10.6% to 11.9%. Order intake grew 6.3% to €327 million, bringing the book-to-bill ratio to 0.86x. Slide bearing sales grew 2.5% to €128 million, with EBIT growing 6.9% to €23 million, indicating a 70-basis point margin expansion to 17.9%. Order intake declined 5% to €126 million with a book-to-bill ratio of around 1.

RENK Group Guides for Around 10% Sales Growth and Margin Expansion

For RENK Group, the defense sales are the growth driver and ramping up production should deliver margin expansion. For 2026, the company guides for more than €1.5 billion in sales, which would indicate around 10% growth in sales. The company is guiding for adjusted EBIT between €255 million and €285 million. This would indicate earnings growth between 10.9% and 24%. The derived margins from sales and earnings guidance would be 17%-19% compared to 16.8% a year ago. So, the company is marching towards its 2030 targets, which are €2.8-€3 billion in sales with margins over 20%, but especially on sales, the company needs to keep delivering year after year.

RENK Group Has Significant Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: Values are converted from EUR to USD. RENK Group has two OTC tickers; the first one is RNKGF, representing the ordinary shares, and the second one is RKGRY, representing 0.2 ordinary shares. The price targets are based on the ordinary shares, meaning that to obtain the price target for RKGRY, you simply have to divide the stated price targets by five. We do note that RNKGF has the higher trading volume. Given that higher volumes and liquidity provide better chances to buy and sell at desired prices and in desired quantities, I do believe that RNKGF provides the better option out of the two to invest in RENK Group. Alternatively, we note that the R3NK ticker in Frankfurt provides even higher volumes.

For RENK Group, I am maintaining my buy rating with a base case target of $74.44, indicating 18% upside, and a more optimistic target of $81.29, indicating 29% upside. We note that this upside is based on expected earnings for 2026. Usually, I value stocks a year ahead of earnings, so in this case that would be by looking at expected earnings and cash flows for 2027. However, for RENK Group, we see that even this year's earnings are not fully reflected in the stock price yet. Expected sales growth is 16.4% annually through 2028, with 21.6% growth in EBITDA and 30.5% growth in free cash flow.

For RENK Group, the expectations are that margins will significantly increase, and that is driven by the company increasing its volumes, which drives operating leverage. In the years ahead, operating and free cash flow will grow. We note that the free cash flow in 2026 benefits from some contracts slipping from 2025 into 2026. CapEx is expected to peak this year at 5% of sales. The company is expected to increase its dividends in the years ahead, but that does not drive an impressive yield. However, over the longer term it could be a good yield on cost, though RENK Group is likely a stock you should buy for the potential upside, and the dividend is just a "nice to have". Net debt is expected to substantially decrease as the company's free cash flow accelerates. In terms of net debt leverage, we see that the company is in a comfortable position.

Conclusion: RENK Group Operating Leverage Should Drive the Business

I believe that RENK Group has bright prospects as the company ramps up its production. Production ramp-ups come with some initial margin downside but ultimately drive operating leverage, and for RENK Group, the improvements should be notable in the EBITDA margin expansion, growing EBITDA, and free cash flows. I believe that the stock remains a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HealthEquity: This Financial Powerhouse Is Poised To Compound Wealth (Upgrade)
data.description:
It’s been a while since I last covered HealthEquity (HQY) in December 2020. I had a "Hold" rating on the stock at the time due primarily to valuation, and that appears to have been a prescient call. Since then, HQY has only delivered a 22% total return, well under the 90% from the S&P 500 (SPY).

In this article, I revisit HQY, including recent business fundamentals, and discuss why now may finally be a good time to layer into this growth stock, so let’s get started!

Why HQY?

HealthEquity operates as the largest independent custodian and administrator of health savings accounts (HSAs) in the United States. At its core, it helps individuals and employers save, spend, and invest funds for healthcare expenses through tax-advantaged accounts.

There are few companies in healthcare that sit at the intersection of structural cost pressure, favorable policy momentum, and scalable financial infrastructure. HealthEquity is one of them. What looks like a steady, low-volatility business is quietly evolving into a high-quality compounder. This has been powered by rising healthcare costs, increased customer engagement with HSAs, and a platform that becomes more valuable with scale.

Notably, HQY now has 17.8 million accounts and over $36 billion in HSA assets. This has created a data-rich ecosystem where engagement, balances, and monetization reinforce each other. To use a flywheel analogy, as engagement deepens, the business model becomes more valuable and efficient.

Recent Performance And Developments

Recent developments reinforce this evolution. HQY saw solid revenue growth of 7% YoY during fiscal Q4 2026 (ended 1/31/26). While this does not appear to be spectacular on the surface, the real story lies in the profitability. HQY’s margin expanded by over 500 basis points, and adjusted EBITDA grew by 23% over the prior year period.

This implies that HQY has reached an inflection point on earnings acceleration. HQY launched a healthcare marketplace that allows users to spend HSA dollars directly on services like weight loss programs, hormone therapy, and wearables. This is more than a feature add. It’s a shift toward capturing a larger share of healthcare spending flows.

Moreover, costs are coming down. Fraud expenses have dropped dramatically, and AI-driven automation is reducing service costs. Notably, fraud reimbursements fell to just $0.3 million in the quarter. The result is a business demonstrating clear operating leverage. As assets grow and engagement increases, incremental revenue is falling through at a much higher margin.

The result is a business demonstrating clear operating leverage. As assets grow and engagement increases, HQY’s incremental revenue is falling through at a much higher margin.

HQY’s Outlook

Looking forward, HQY has a long growth runway, as 95% of HSA members are not contributing the maximum to their HSAs and over 90% are not investing in HSAs at all. HQY is also seeing a shift toward higher-value accounts over time. Older cohorts accumulate larger balances and generate more revenue for the company, creating a compounding effect.

At the same time, new distribution channels are opening up. A key development is the expansion of HSA eligibility to individuals on ACA exchange Bronze plans. This effectively broadens the addressable market beyond employer-sponsored plans. Management noted the current environment as being the most constructive in two decades during the recent earnings call:

The policy environment for HSAs is the most constructive it has been in 2 decades. And we believe we are at a genuine inflection point with health care affordability at the center of the conversation.

Last year's Working Families Tax Cuts Act, also known as One Big Beautiful Bill, expanded HSA eligibility to American selecting Bronze plans offered on ACA exchanges. This law is the most significant structural change to the HSA market since the accounts were created.

For the first time, millions of households purchasing coverage through the exchanges compare their plans with an HSA and access the same triple tax advantages that employer sponsor participants have long benefited. This is a meaningful democratization of a tool that has historically skewed towards employer-covered workers.

Risks

Interest rates remain a key variable. A portion of revenue is tied to yields on HSA cash balances, and a prolonged decline in rates would pressure custodial revenue. While the company has hedging strategies in place, this remains an external factor outside management’s control.

There is also behavioral risk. The growth thesis depends heavily on increasing engagement, contributions, and investment activity. If consumers fail to adopt these behaviors at the expected pace, growth could underperform.

Competition is another consideration. While HealthEquity has strong retention and scale advantages, banks and other financial institutions still participate in the HSA ecosystem.

HQY currently has a sub-investment grade credit rating of BB from S&P. However, management is taking steps to deleverage, as HQY’s debt was reduced by 10% over the past 12 months, from $1.06 billion to $957 million.

Valuation

I believe HQY offers compelling value at the current price of $83.67 with a forward P/E of 18.1, sitting far under its historical P/E of 53.1, as shown below.

The recent earnings report makes a strong case that this is no longer just a “nice story” stock. It is increasingly a business with visible operating leverage, durable growth drivers, and multiple expansion pathways that the market may still be underestimating. Analysts estimate 15%-16% annual EPS growth over the next two years, which makes the current valuation seem inexpensive. As such, I believe HQY is poised to deliver double-digit annual returns from here.

Investor Takeaway

HealthEquity is not a typical high-growth story. It does not rely on explosive top-line expansion or disruptive technology narratives. Instead, it is building something quieter but potentially more durable through platform expansion and customer adoption.

This is a platform that benefits from time, scale, and behavior. Accounts mature, balances grow, engagement deepens, and margins expand. Each of these reinforces the others.

The recent earnings confirm that the model is working. The forward outlook suggests that multiple tailwinds are aligning. And the underlying opportunity remains largely untapped. Considering all the above, I upgrade HQY from a "Hold" to a "Buy."

data.title: FDA Peptide Review, A Big Moment For Hims & Hers (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis: Upside Going Into H2-2026

The big news concerning Hims & Hers (HIMS) this week has been the announcement of a set of upcoming July advisory meetings led by the FDA. Some investors are clearly viewing this as a pivotal regulatory moment for compounded peptide therapies, particularly GLP-1 drugs like semaglutide. It seems the agency is reassessing whether certain compounds can actually be safely produced outside traditional approval pathways despite prior restrictions. We obviously have approved therapies from companies like Novo Nordisk (NVO), which have had to demonstrate tightly controlled pharmacokinetics, reproducible receptor activation, and validated long-term outcomes to get approval. However, compounded alternatives have basically done extremely well on unmet demand and accessibility, even though there is a lot of scientific and regulatory uncertainty around consistency and safety.

It would then mean the bull case is that if the FDA ultimately adopts a controlled but permissive framework, in that they allow select peptides to be compounded under stricter quality/sourcing standards, it would then essentially legitimise a parallel supply channel for Hims. This would then unlock renewed growth for Hims and allow it to reintroduce or expand its GLP-1 offerings, which helped the stock price hit multiple peaks across 2025. I think in that scenario, the market’s current discount on revenue multiples could reverse since there will be regulatory clarity to help reduce the risk and also validate Hims’ role as a high-growth distribution platform. However, that being said, the flip side case would see the FDA using this regulatory assessment to reinforce their prior ban. In that they use this to build a much stronger case against the use of compounded versions of GLP-1 and put to rest a lot of noise from parties calling for a review. So there’s obviously a lot of risk here, but I do think we could see the stock gaining more upside momentum going into the meetings and post-meetings in 2H26. In my previous coverage, I went over Hims’ last earnings release back when the stock was in the mid-$14 range. The stock has seen considerable upside since then, with shares now trading in the high-$26 range pre-market today.

FDA Meetings This July

As you probably heard, the FDA has decided to convene an advisory committee, which should make up a pretty important turning point in how peptide-based therapies are regulated. This would be especially for the fast-growing compounding pharmacy sector. You see, they’re set to revisit their 2023 move to restrict around 14 peptides, and now the FDA is signalling some kind of openness to re-evaluating whether some of those specific compounds can actually be safely produced outside traditional drug approval pathways.

What got my attention in particular was that they're set to involve the agency’s Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee, which would suggest a much more evidence-driven approach. These products have surged dramatically in popularity, even though there’s been quite limited large-scale clinical validation. I think one big driver here has been online health influencers and anecdotal endorsements. There’s also been quite a bit of political pressure, with the likes of Robert F. Kennedy Jr. publicly advocating for fewer restrictions in place here. He’s also framed peptides as beneficial based on personal experience. Eventually, what it means is that the FDA is set to hold meetings on the 23rd and 24th of July specifically to focus on seven peptides, while another five peptides will be looked at in detail before February next year.

So the outcome of the July meetings could have a lot of meaningful implications for pharma and biotech companies such as Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo, which have all but dominated approved metabolic and peptide-based therapies. But also some firms like Hims could catch more favourable tailwinds here, in that a bull-case outcome would see them expand their offerings if compounding rules loosen. However, Hims has just revisited its partnership with Novo to list its GLP-1 products, so can we expect Hims to relist a compounded product if the outcome of the FDA meetings is favourable? Ultimately, I see the FDA as having a lot on its plate here; they have to balance the rising consumer demand and also all the innovation against unresolved concerns about safety, efficacy, and quality control. Which is why the market saw it as such an inflection point for Hims, which closed about 14% higher yesterday and is up another 10% in pre-market hours today, both regulators and the broader peptide market.

Meeting Implications: Buying In Now Is High Risk

Now, I don’t think we should view this as the advisory committee simply debating if peptides should be allowed, but rather they’ll be analysing a lot of technical layers. I think they’ll be fundamentally centered on whether compounded GLP-1 peptides, most notably semaglutide, can be considered pharmacologically and chemically equivalent to their FDA-approved counterparts. As for that, the answer would then hinge on some subtle but pretty critical scientific distinctions. As you know, approved semaglutide, commercialised by the likes of Novo, is a highly engineered 31-amino-acid peptide analogue of endogenous GLP-1. So that includes specific structural modifications, such as a fatty acid side-chain attachment, which is there to increase albumin binding but also extend its half-life to roughly one week. It gives us a stable, but more importantly, predictable activation of the GLP-1 receptor. It also comes with a lot of tightly characterised pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics.

Now, if we put that in contrast with compounded versions, these may use what we call different salt forms, such as semaglutide sodium or acetate, which have somewhat variable synthesis pathways. They also have non-standard excipients, any of which can actually alter peptide folding, aggregation behaviour, and thus receptor binding affinity. Which is why the FDA was so unfavourable on them previously. You see, even minor deviations in peptide purity or sequence integrity can start to introduce inactive fragments, misfolded isoforms, or even immunogenic contaminants that can then change how the molecule interacts with the GLP-1 receptor or how long it can actually persist in circulation. So with that being said, I expect the FDA to scrutinise not just the nominal likeness of the active ingredient but to dig into deeper issues such as chirality accuracy, peptide chain truncation, even oxidation states, and batch-to-batch reproducibility. Now, other than that, we can also expect real-world delivery differences like vial-based dosing to be looked at. This would then bring into question a lot of the variability in administered dose and exposure versus the prefilled auto-injectors used in approved drugs. I see this part creating a mechanistic gap, in that both compounded and branded products do still aim to activate the same receptor pathway to enhance insulin secretion, then suppress appetite, and slow the gastric emptying, but only the approved versions have validated, reproducible exposure-response relationships and long-term safety data. So what puts compounded versions at a big disadvantage would be the lack of data.

The implications for Hims here are pretty significant because if the FDA concludes that compounded semaglutide cannot reliably replicate the molecular consistency and systemic behaviour of the approved drug, it could then use that review as a solid backing to go on to tighten/eliminate the regulatory pathway that currently enables lower-cost, high-demand GLP-1 offerings. It would cut a key growth driver for Hims as a result. But on the other hand, if a controlled framework is established that accepts certain compounded peptides, albeit under strict quality and sourcing standards, it would essentially legitimise a parallel supply channel and then reinforce Hims’ position as a distribution layer for those metabolic therapies. So even though it is very early days to be looking at a potential outcome, with Hims' share price being low compared to where it was previously when compounded semaglutide was selling well, a lot of investors are choosing to take on this risk simply because the potential upside here is so big.

Chief Medical Officer Pat Carroll On The News

We’ve also seen Hims’ Chief Medical Officer Pat Carroll positioning more so aligned with the FDA’s push to formalise peptide regulation. He’s framing the agency’s efforts more so as a step toward legitimising and even professionalising what has largely been a grey-market space for the past few years. So he is clearly bullish on the review, and the company has emphasised a physician-led, safety-first approach, as we would expect. It seems they want compounded semaglutide, but they also know the importance of framing here, in that they have to remove the negative connotations about compounded peptides and bring a more scientific safety approach. They also have a lot of intent to expand access to peptide therapies, again framing this to be within clearer regulatory boundaries and maintaining standards around clinical transparency. But for me, this messaging pretty much signals that Hims is preparing to operate within a potential stricter FDA guidance rather than circumvent it. They, of course, want to set themselves up as a trusted, scalable platform if the regulatory landscape is going to evolve.

Valuation

Hims is currently trading at a valuation that would look superficially inexpensive on a revenue basis but actually quite demanding on profitability metrics. I think the market here is trying to price in some strong future growth and recovery but also remaining very cautious on execution risk. The company holds an EV-to-sales ratio of about 2.0x forward and a price-to-sales ratio of 1.8x forward, both of which are at about a 40% to 50% discount to the healthcare sector. So this would suggest that investors still view Hims as a relatively cheap growth story in digital health. This would be particularly true when we consider their expanding subscription base and scaling revenue model. However, this is pretty much offset by some much richer earnings multiples. The forward price-to-earnings is nearing 39x, and EV-to-EBITDA is still above sector averages, which would imply profitability remains a key concern here.

So that valuation gap tells me the market is essentially discounting near-term margin pressure and regulatory uncertainty, including areas like compounded treatments such as peptides, while also trying to price in a sharp earnings inflection over the next 2 to 4 years, which we can see with the consensus EPS growth accelerating significantly into 2027 to 2029. In other words, I feel Hims is being valued less like a mature healthcare company and more like a hybrid consumer-tech platform here, with it being cheap on sales because of scepticism around durability and regulation but still pretty expensive on earnings because investors expect operating leverage and monetisation to improve meaningfully if their growth initiatives play out.

Looking Ahead

So, looking ahead, the July meetings from the FDA should likely serve as a pretty key inflection point, and not just for peptide regulation. It will be more so for how scalable and defensible alternative GLP-1 distribution models really are. As for Hims, the path forward from here would then hinge on whether regulation evolves to accommodate those controlled compounding methods or moves in a harsher direction to simply reinforce strict approval pathways. The latter here would essentially limit access and see a lot of downside for Hims, which adds to the risk here. So until we see that clearly, I think the stock should stay as a high-beta expression of regulatory risk. The upside is tied to policy flexibility, but the downside here reflects the possibility that the current growth narrative we’re seeing is more fragile than the market is willing to admit.

data.title: CGDV: Retirees Can Use The 4% Rule On This Dividend ETF
data.description:
Overview

You've probably heard the traditional retirement advice of using the 4% withdrawal rate. This requires retirees to sell off 4% of their portfolio every year to help support their lifestyle expenses. This is a strategy that is most commonly implemented on an ETF that tracks the total stock market or S&P 500. However, I would argue that the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) is a better choice for retirees following this 4% rule. The fund outperforms during rallies, market pullbacks, and periods of choppiness. The fund implements a strategy that has demonstrated success in preserving capital through periods of uncertainty as well.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CGDV's share price has increased by nearly 32.4%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to provide impressive returns, even through periods of market uncertainty. When including all dividends paid to investors, the total return jumps a bit higher to 34.5% over the same time frame. The fund's starting dividend yield of 1.2% isn't that impressive. While the dividend growth history is a bit more impressive, the fund's short operating history makes it too soon to definitively confirm that a high rate of dividend growth should be expected going forward.

When I previously covered CGDV, I issued a buy rating because of the fund's outperformance against the S&P 500 Index (SPX). Since then, the fund has indeed continued to outperform the S&P 500 through all of the uncertainty. Looking forward, I believe that this outperformance is likely to continue, even in a scenario where the technology sector rapidly appreciates higher. I specifically wanted to revisit CGDV to discuss its value proposition, outlook, and appeal for retirees. The fund has achieved a unique dynamic where it captures more upside growth than the indices while limiting downside risks. So if you are a retiree using the 4% rule, it makes sense to hold a lower beta ETF.

Standout Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, CGDV has total assets under management of $31.5B. This is a significant increase from the total AUM of $24.5B at the time of my last coverage. This can be a clear indication that investors are piling into the fund as its strategy continues to prove efficient. The fund's primary goal is to produce income exceeding the average yield on U.S. stocks. However, the focus on dividends here is a bit unique compared to other dividend-focused ETFs.

In reality, I believe that the fund is more aligned with an objective of maximizing total return potential due to some of the eligibility requirements. For instance, the fund only holds companies that have a strong balance sheet and whose debt is rated as investment grade. Furthermore, management focuses on dividend-paying companies, as well as companies with the potential to pay out dividends. The key thing here is that CGDV includes companies that have the potential to pay dividends, which likely means these companies have healthy free cash flows and display attractive earnings growth.

Furthermore, CGDV is actively managed, and it does not try to replicate any specific index. CGDV does use the Russell 1000 as its performance index, though. The fund also takes a more focused approach and only has 52 holdings. Since it is actively managed, the fund has a higher expense ratio of 0.33% of assets. However, I think this expense ratio is worth it considering the potential for outperformance.

Looking at the breakdown of the portfolio by sector, we can see that CGDV is most heavily allocated to technology investments at 29.47% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to industrial and health care investments, which accounts for 14.94% and 12.51% of assets, respectively. The meaningful inclusion of technology companies is noteworthy because many dividend ETFs fail to include the technology sector. The reality is that there aren't many technology companies with a long track record of dividend growth. However, CGDV's selection process opens up its portfolio to a wider range of choices.

When looking at the holdings, there is one thing that stands out to me. CGDV doesn't have any arbitrary requirement for how long a company had to increase its dividend payouts. This means that technology companies with a short dividend history but strong cash flows can be included. For instance, NVIDIA (NVDA) is the second-largest position at 5.43% of assets. This is accompanied by a mix of other high-quality companies, including Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), Applied Materials (AMAT), RTX Corporation (RTX), and Meta Platforms (META), just to name a few.

Lastly, the fund also provides a small allocation to companies that are based outside of the U.S. 92.5% of the fund is still allocated to U.S. positions, so this shouldn't be confused as a dedicated global fund. Instead, CGDV sprinkles in a bit of extra diversity across Canada, Europe, and the Middle East. Furthermore, the ETF has exposure to companies that generate revenues from Asia and emerging markets as well.

Performance Drivers

CGDV's outperformance ultimately comes down to a few factors. The primary driver of CGDV's strong performance is the fact that it holds large-cap businesses that pay a small dividend. Many other dividend ETFs tend to have an arbitrary requirement around a company's dividend yield. For instance, there are many dividend ETFs that don't include a stock if the yield isn't above 3% or doesn't have an established history of dividend raises over time. Since CGDV doesn't have these requirements, the fund includes strong growth positions like Meta Platforms, Nvidia, Apple (AAPL), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), and Alphabet (GOOG). These companies do in fact pay a dividend, but their yields are small, and they don't have the strongest growth history.

Furthermore, the fund provides exposure to companies like Amazon (AMZN) and Uber Technologies (UBER). These companies both fall under that category of having the potential to issue dividends. By including all of these rapidly growing companies, the fund is capable of participating in the upside movements of the market. For instance, we can measure the efficiency of this approach when observing CGDV's performance since its inception.

For instance, CGDV's total return now sits at 96.7%, which is very impressive in such a short time span. Additionally, CGDV outpaces more traditional growth ETFs, such as the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), the Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund (VTI), and Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). CGDV's ability to outperform QQQ should be commended and taken as a clear indication that the strategy is efficient.

2026 was off to a rough start as capital rotated out of technology and into other areas of the market. The rapid growth of AI has caused some uncertainty about the sustainability of the moat for many SaaS companies. As the indices sold off, CGDV was able to preserve capital a bit better. For instance, CGDV's total return sits around 4.3% on a YTD basis, which proves that the fund was capable of preserving capital a lot better than the more common ETFs.

Lastly, CGDV is one of the few dividend funds that is positioned to participate in the expansion of the AI market. Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can reach a value of ~$3.5T by 2033. This would indicate a potential CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% over the next few years. Even if this estimate were off by a 50% margin, this would still indicate a potential double-digit upside for the AI market. So if AI does drive the indices higher in the near term, CGDV will be capable of participating in this growth.

Dividend Utility for Retirees

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.1067 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 1.2%. The starting dividend yield is only slightly higher than what SPY offers, so CGDV isn't the traditional type of high-yield dividend ETF. In order to get a meaningful income stream from CGDV, it would require several millions of dollars invested. Even with $5M invested into the fund, the annual dividend income would land somewhere around $60,000 annually, which is still not enough for a quality retirement in many places across the U.S. Additionally, the payout amounts have historically varied every quarter, which isn't a good fit for investors that want a consistent stream of income.

$5M invested @ a 1.2% dividend yield = ~$60,000 annually

On a more positive note, the fund has maintained a healthy track record of dividend growth. For instance, CGDV has been able to achieve a dividend CAGR of 14.18% over the last three years. I do believe that CGDV's strategy and holdings can lead to a sustainable high single-digit to low double-digit annual growth over a longer holding period. The only issue is that the fund's yield is so low that it will still take a lot of time for an investor's yield on cost to substantially rise. For instance, investors that bought CGDV on the day of its inception would only have a yield on cost of around 2.2%.

Therefore, the fund is best utilized by the retiree that doesn't mind selling shares. The dividends received from CGDV are a bonus, but the fund's ability to outperform market indices is where the real value lies. Traditional retirement advice tells us to withdraw 4% of the portfolio to fuel your lifestyle expenses. This advice is most commonly followed by folks that buy an S&P 500 fund. I would argue that CGDV is a more efficient holding for this 4% withdrawal rate since the fund offers outperformance with less volatility.

Furthermore, CGDV is great for retirees because of the tax efficiency. The fund's year-end breakdown reveals that 100% of the distributions paid out during 2025 were classified as qualified dividends, which have the most favorable tax treatment for investors. Therefore, the fund provides a bit of flexibility in terms of which account type investors can utilize a position in.

Risks

While I am optimistic about the outlook of technology and AI, this potential strength can also be a risk for the fund. We've already seen how quickly market sentiment can shift for the AI market. Investors are selling off SaaS companies, and there are still concerns around the massive levels of capex spending from the hyperscalers.

The market is questioning how these large investments will actually translate into a higher baseline earnings for many of the large-cap leaders. So if we experience a scenario where the market experiences a downturn or growing pessimism around AI, a selloff of the sector would negatively impact CGDV and threaten its ability to outperform against the indices. In this scenario, CGDV is likely to underperform against other dividend ETFs that have a higher allocation to defensive sectors, such as energy or utilities.

For instance, the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) has a sizeable allocation to health care, consumer defensive, and energy companies. While the technology sector experienced a rough start to 2026, the energy sector experienced a strong rally because of the rising power demands. Since SCHD was heavily allocated to energy companies at the time, it was able to provide a total return that significantly outperformed CGDV on a YTD basis.

Furthermore, the fund has not set a rebalancing or reconstitution schedule. Therefore, investors are entirely reliant on management's ability to select high-quality holdings and to quickly rotate out of positions that may be underperforming. Just because the fund's management hasn't disappointed yet, there's always a chance that they misread an opportunity within its portfolio.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on CGDV at this time. The fund has proven its ability to participate in greater upside movements of the market while protecting capital during periods of market declines. Although the fund's name implies a specific focus on dividend-paying companies, it also includes companies that have the potential to pay dividends. This leads the fund to include quality growth companies, such as Amazon, which has healthy free cash flow levels and excellent earnings growth. Since the fund has a sizeable allocation to technology companies, the fund is aligned to participate in the expansion of the AI market. For retirees, the yield may not be compelling, but the excellent total return makes this a great fund to implement the 4% rule on.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: Exceptional Business, Stretched Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) is one of the world's most critical firms in all things technology. The fundamental developments have largely reflected this fact as of late, and the stock has followed. It is certain to say that investors now have their expectations heightened, as the stock has rebounded drastically from the 2025 tariff-induced lows, significantly outperforming the S&P 500 index (SPX), which has already performed very well since then itself.

TSM is the world’s leading pure-play semiconductor foundry, manufacturing advanced chips for fabless designers and integrated device makers for smartphones, high-performance computing, automotive, and other markets. In recent years, the business has become even more concentrated in leading-edge technologies and AI-driven demand, as HPC is gaining an increased share of total revenues. That shift portrays that Taiwan Semiconductor is not just a smartphone-linked cyclical foundry, but increasingly the backbone of the AI compute buildout.

Rapidly rising revenues and accompanying margin expansion confirm that. This is where the bullish thesis on TSM comes in. The current AI buildout could last longer than expected, and current business momentum for TSM may be maintained for a couple of years. Data points such as Anthropic reaching $30B ARR mid-year despite anticipating $26B for 2026 in a bullish scenario are in support of this. In that case the stock would likely continue to outperform. However, I believe that TSM's extended valuation largely reflects the above already, leaving little upside edge when entering now.

The company reported their Q1 2026 earnings today. Let's check the most recent developments and take a look at current valuation.

Financials

TSM's earnings slides make it apparent which sectors represent what share of total revenues. HPC (this is where AI falls into) holds the biggest share at 61%. Smartphone and automotive actually shrank YoY, by 11% and 7%, respectively. Taiwan Semiconductor can therefore report strong financials even with some business lines performing weakly. This can also be read in a more risk-sensitive way, as it could be an indication that TSM remains cyclical, and if AI revenues start to slow down, then the stock could significantly underperform.

Reading the income statement should be pleasant for current investors, as everything seems to be growing rapidly. Revenues grew 40.6% YoY and 6.4% QoQ. Gross margin reached 66.2%, far above guidance of 64%. Similarly, operating margins climbed to 58.1%, above guidance of 55%. EPS grew 58.3% YoY and 13.2% QoQ. Fundamentally, the company is completely intact.

Looking at the balance sheet, cash and marketable securities together represent almost 40% of total assets and by themselves top total liabilities. TSM is a company that prints cash, and as such, continues to have a pristine balance sheet.

Operating cash flows grew just 12% YoY, which is due to "Other operating sources (Uses)" being negative this year and positive in the same quarter last year. This is a development that should be monitored, as operating cash flow should rise along with revenue, if not quicker. Free cash flow may grow slower, if the company is in an investment cycle. Capital expenditures for TSMC on a consolidated basis totaled $11.10B in 1Q26, leading to free cash flow of 348B NT$, which represents 18% growth YoY.

For the second quarter of 2026, TSM guides for revenues between $39B and $40.2B. Gross margins are expected to be around 66.5% and operating margins at 57.5%. However, I do believe that they will reach the upper end of all their guidance again, if not beyond that, given their earnings surprise history.

Valuation

TSM's P/E ratio seems stretched when comparing it to its 10-year minimum at 11.3, maximum at 39.3, and average at 22.1. At 35.8, it is close to the maximum and around 90% above its average.

The picture looks similar with their P/OCF ratio, though here we are essentially at the maximum of the historical range at 27. This is nearly double the 10-year average of 13.7. To me, this is somewhat concerning, as OCF is not impacted by rising capital expenditures to meet demand and therefore not artificially lowered at the moment. This means the company is actually more expensive than ever, judging from this metric.

While TSM certainly deserves part of this premium in the trailing multiples over its own history given its AI prospects, I do believe that it is best to buy high-quality companies at relatively lower valuations, particularly in periods of fear and doubt, such as in April 2025, and then hold them for the long term.

Looking at analysts' expectations, EPS is expected to grow between 20% and 40% annually for the coming years. This is ambitious but achievable given TSM's current business momentum. Still, this development would merely lead to price appreciation potential of roughly 11% per year when applying its average P/E of 22.1 to 2028's figures. This is solid and makes a clear case to hold, but not quite worthy of a buy rating in my opinion.

At the same time, geopolitical tensions are increasing. The Strait of Hormuz remains severely disrupted, with shipping traffic far below normal levels, while broader Middle East conflict continues to threaten energy markets and global risk sentiment, and still, markets are back to all-time highs. The invasion risk of Taiwan from China remains real and needs to be discounted on the stock. Some discounting is certainly already applied, as if TSM were a US company, its P/E ratio would likely be closer to 60 or 70.

Conclusion

Putting it all together, it is clear that TSM is on a great trajectory qualitatively, and the numbers support that. The market already knows about a lot of these developments, however, which is clearly reflected in its rich valuation. Q1 2026 figures underscored that strong business momentum remains intact, but heightened valuations increase execution and downside risk. Still, the stock is not a sell for me, because it is potentially one of the best ways to maintain AI upside exposure, being a well-established and very profitable foundry play.

Rating: Initiate at Hold.

data.title: Snap: Cutting The Bloat
data.description:
Snap Overview

Under increasing shareholder pressure, Snap, Inc. (SNAP) was pushed into restructuring the business to vastly lower expenses. The social messaging company is hopefully entering a year of efficiency similar to other moves made by more prominent social media companies to great success. My investment thesis is ultra bullish on the stock due to the cheap valuation and enhanced profit outlook.

Restructure Plan

Snap announced plans to cut 1,000 jobs and eliminate 300 open positions. The move will reduce the annualized cost base by more than $500 million by the 2H'26, helping to establish a higher level of profitability.

The company is increasingly under pressure from activists to improve financial results. Irenic Capital announced a 2.5% position in Snap and released a 70 page presentation offering a path for the stock to rally nearly 7x to $26 from the recent lows at $4.

Snap ended 2025 with Q4 net income of only $45 million, while adjusted EBITDA was $358 million. The company reported quarterly FCF of $206 million, so the financial picture was far more positive than the market view, but Snap should have annual adjusted EBITDA far above $689 million, or just 11.6% margins.

The company guided Q1'26 revenues up to $1.529 billion, above consensus analyst estimates of $1.52 billion. Management is forecasting growth of 12%, which again shows the great stock disconnect with results.

Snap has already reached the $1 billion level in subscriptions and had another $400 million deal with Perplexity AI. The business had a lot of positives for a stock trading near all-time lows.

The market just couldn't get over the cost structure, led by stock-based compensation. Snap reported 2025 SBC of $1.0 billion and guided to a 2026 charge of $1.2 billion, but the share count was only up 3% last year to 1.88 billion shares.

The combination of the forecasted 13% sales growth (~$800 million in additional revenues), 60% gross margins, and a $500 million reduction in operating expenses leads to adjusted EBITDA much closer to $1.5 billion going forward.

Snap will report Q1 earnings on May 6 and hopefully provide a lot more clarity on the AI plans, considering the issues with Perplexity AI and the opportunity with Specs. Management can't just kick the issues down the road anymore.

Activist Pressure

The social media stock plunged to below $4 on fears over social media lawsuits combined with the stock market weakness due to the Iran conflict. Snap has seen a quick turnaround due to a big call by Irenic Capital with a $26 price target.

Irenic made the following proposal with a big focus on shutting down the Specs business, which is losing an estimated $500 million annually, and improving governance. The activist even promotes a $5.8 billion share buyback based on using half the cash flows from adjusted EBITDA over the next 3 years.

The Specs division is planning the launch of a consumer product this year. Considering the smart glasses segment is heating up, Snap clearly should work towards a viable path to profits or spin out the division. Right now, Snap doesn't have the scale or push to fully monetize this division despite having a promising product.

Clearly, Snap needs activist pressure to improve monetization and rationalize costs. The solution to all of the problems is a growing subscription business and a replacement for the Perplexity AI product, which promised $400 million in annual revenues and expansion into additional AI opportunities.

SNAP stock could rise to the $26 price target based solely on executing on these 2 monetization opportunities. This $500 million cost structure improvement and stripping out the $500 million in annual costs from Specs could add even more value.

Irenic assigns Snap a $26 PT based only on a 9x EV/EBITDA multiple based on reaching a $3.87 billion EBITDA amount. The multiple is a big discount from where such a business would normally trade; at least 15x EV/EBITDA would be typical.

Snap has to make dramatic improvement in adjusted EBITDA to go from a $1.05 billion goal for 2026 to reach $3.87 billion. The improved cost structure solves a lot of the problems and gets to $2+ billion in adjusted EBITDA with the rest needing to come from higher monetization.

The social messaging company needs higher ad monetization to match peers, but one simple path to more revenues and higher margins is hiking subscription revenues. Our prior research highlights how media players like Netflix (NFLX) have routinely hiked subscription prices, and Snapchat+ could easily double from a current price of only $26.99/year, or a very minimal $2.25/month.

The big question is whether Snap could buy so many shares to reduce the share count by nearly 600 million to reach the 1.3 billion goal, especially if the stock price rises much higher. The company did end last quarter with a cash balance of $2.9 billion, and Snap will be very cash flow positive now.

The stock would have a value closer to $58 billion at 15x adjusted EBITDA targets. Even with 1.5 billion shares outstanding, Snap would have a price target of $39.

The biggest risk is that Snap just continues floundering. Even with targeted 13% revenue growth this year and a booming subscription business, management was struggling to incorporate an AI monetization strategy, with the Perplexity deal over 3 months late and the Specs business having no visible plan. The stock could easily be dead money from here and dip back to the $4 lows on a lack of execution in an AI world.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Snap got far too cheap at the lows. The stock still only trades at less than 2x sales targets for 2026.

Investors should continue loading up at $6, with Snap having multiple catalysts for growth and the ability to further lower costs to dramatically boost cash flows. The investor base has long overfocused on SBC costs that lead to limited share dilution, but improvements in lowering costs will nonetheless help the stock.

data.title: Tractor Supply: Inflation On The Way To Support Comps
data.description:
Tractor Supply Company (TSCO) earnings are just around the corner on April 21, and the stock has come in a decent amount in the last few months, setting up for a positive risk skew. The last year has not been the company's best, with comps barely positive and margins compressing on a higher fixed cost base. Inflation has been nascent, which the company needs to help drive comp store sales. Fortunately for the company, I believe the most challenging days are behind them, and recent geopolitical tensions bode well for comp store sales. With the stock trading at a historically attractive valuation, I think it's a good time to step into this name.

Business Analysis

If you're new to Tractor Supply, it's essentially the farm and ranch version of The Home Depot, Inc. (HD) or Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW). It's primarily more rural real estate, but with an interesting twist—the company has nearly half of its sales coming from what it calls CUE. CUE is just consumable products; whether it's hay for horses or wood pellets for stoves, they have the everyday farmer and gardener covered. While HD and LOW have more exposure to big and bulky items (lumber, rock, gravel, furniture), TSCO tries to capitalize on the staple demand profile of farmers who have consistent needs for inputs for animals and taking care of their farms.

The company has been struggling in the past few years to get any sort of real, sustained comp store sales momentum. In its latest quarter, they put up just 0.3% on top of 0.6% last year, versus an algorithm that calls for LSD%, seen below from their 2024 Investor Day. On paper, this is a great algorithm. If you unpack the comp store sales growth, you're looking at 1-2 points of volume per year with 1-2 points of ticket per year, largely justified by perpetually rising costs for TSCO and other mainline retailers like wages, rent, and utilities. To reach the 5% upper level on comp, discretionary purchases need to be happening, which can help to produce a double-digit return profile for the stock on a forward basis.

Source: Investor Presentation.

Financials & Balance Sheet Assessment

Despite a solid algorithm, Tractor Supply has been slowing in recent quarters while opening more stores than ever—99 in their latest quarter, with five PetSense stores (a small, immaterial banner they own). Comps were 1.2% in 2025, driven by traffic of 1.4% and tickets down 0.2%, and inflation was tough to come by in CUE.

Profitability continues to erode at Tractor Supply. As of Q4, operating margins were down 80 bps y/y, and net income was down nearly 5%. For TSCO stock to start to work, there needs to be 1) comp store sales inflection/better than estimates, 2) forthcoming inflation to aid margins, and 3) SG&A leverage. Their planned strategic initiatives have hurt their SG&A profile with nearly 60 bps of deleverage in the past year.

TSCO is about to report Q1 earnings. Recall the guide:

  • Net sales +4-6%.
  • SSS% (comparable store sales) +1-3%.
  • Operating margin 9.3%-9.6%.
  • Net income $1.11-$1.17bn.
  • Diluted EPS $2.13-$2.23bn.
  • Capex $675mn-$725mn.
  • Share repo $375mn-$450mn.

At the midpoint of guidance, with $2.18 in EPS, that's MSD% growth—below their stated algorithm of 8-11%. TSCO has a long history of beating estimates and surpassing analyst expectations in the current quarter, but it's often from forward revisions with updated guidance that this company gets into trouble and forces the stock lower on the day of the print. The company's average EPS beat tends to be about 5%, with the median about 2%. Misses have been rare over the past several years but have been too common in the last year as comps have slowed.

Source: Passage Research.

Given that earnings are upcoming, there are two big questions to answer: 1) Can TSCO beat earnings/show comp improvement this quarter? And 2) is there inflation on the way such that the forward twelve-month outlook is supportive of an acceleration in growth for both the top and bottom lines?

For our first question, the answer is that history is on the side of the company beating earnings, but without high-frequency data (largely exclusive to institutional investors), it's difficult to tell if comps are set up to beat. Retail sales specifically for home improvement through February have inflected positively, which bodes well for sequential improvement in comps for Tractor versus street estimates looking for 1.8%, in line with that thinking. Estimates should largely be achievable this quarter, but again, the focus should be on forthcoming inflation, as that is what will drive earnings power.

Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

That brings us to question 2. One of the most interesting things about this stock is that it correlates to corn, and corn prices essentially have a 3-month lead time on TSCO stock because of the lead time on comps. In fact, this is best exhibited by the correlation on a 1Y basis between TSCO stock and corn of 81.5%, while the 5Y is about 69.6%. Corn, soybeans, and wheat are the three most important crops for farmers in the US, and TSCO caters to those individuals, which is why the relationship exists.

Source: Compiled by Author.

Due to the ongoing war in Iran, corn prices are now up nearly 5% YTD and have been flat in the last year. That's compared to being down close to 15% y/y when we started the year and providing a deflationary input to TSCO's CUE business. Now that corn prices are up, TSCO could see an increase in comps. To be clear, I wouldn't ascribe more than a few points of comp to something like this, but considering they're starting from a base of near 0% comps, something is better than nothing to help propel earnings power higher.

Valuation

With the free cash flow yield now approaching over 3% and closer to 3.5% on a forward basis, the valuation on this stock is really starting to look attractive on multiple fronts. EV/EBITDA, in particular, is pointing to a stock that is trading a standard deviation below its average, and there appears to be a decent amount of support at this level over the last five years.

Source: Passage Research.

If we assume TSCO can hit the midpoint of guidance at $2.18 and appropriately detail out an on-algo year in 2027, then we're looking at implied forward EPS of $2.38. That means that the stock is trading just below 19x. If comps can pick up from here, it's easily going to get a ~low-20s multiple, and that's before we consider real earnings drivers from here (i.e., higher inflation next year).

Source: Passage Research.

I like the stock here to about a PT of $57, which is a low-20s multiple against $2.50 in earnings next year, as I expect inflation will help support comps back to 3%+ and SG&A will see some leverage.

Risks

TSCO is a home improvement classified retailer and should exhibit shallower drawdowns in demand through the cycle than other retailers, but it is simply not without risk. A lack of an inflation pickup from here, which helps to boost the ticker, would prove to be a considerable headwind for comp store sales growth and leveraging the fixed cost base. That base continues to increase in density as the company increases its store count. Consumer confidence is also another key risk. Given that consumer confidence is hovering near its post-COVID low, the appetite for the consumer to spend remains subdued, and that adversely impacts Tractor in higher-margin parts of the store, particularly apparel and seasonal. Should comps turn negative in a sustained manner from here, we could be looking at a mid-teens multiple and downside risk on the stock to the high 30s.

Conclusion

TSCO is due to report earnings next week, and while this quarter looks like more of the same as past quarters and the company could eke out a small beat with guidance largely unchanged, the forward outlook looks more compelling with the commodity complex picking up in terms of price. This can help to spur a ticket driver in their CUE business, which should force some comp momentum.

data.title: Boeing's Shaping Up, But It's Too Early To Hop On Board
data.description:
Back in November of last year, I decided to take a fresh look at airplane manufacturing giant The Boeing Company (BA). For years, the company has faced certain struggles. Operational inefficiencies, combined with some high profile air related incidents, severely negatively affected financial performance. Last year, the company started showing some meaningful signs of improvement. As backlog grew, revenue increased. The bottom line for the company became healthier as well, and recently management has been successful in reducing overall leverage.

Today, shares actually look much cheaper in some respects than they have over the last few years. However, problems still persist. Even though management is expecting positive free cash flow this year, the stock is nowhere near what I would consider to be cheap considering the issues that it has faced. And yet, the stock is up 14.5% since I reaffirmed it as a 'hold' candidate last November. Over the same window of time, the market is up 3.8%. However, the return disparity is smaller if we look at the time I upgraded it to a 'hold' in June of last year. Since then, shares are up 12.6%, which is actually a bit inferior to the 17.2% rise that the S&P 500 saw.

Long term, I have no doubt that the company would do fine from an operational standpoint. However, I am not ready to upgrade it just yet. Although management is making some significant progress, I want to see more before becoming bullish on it. The good news is that there will be the opportunity for management to prove itself. I say this because, just around the corner, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue is expected to improve, though profitability is forecast to worsen. Naturally, investors should keep a close eye on what goes on here, and they should pay attention to other important details like backlog and debt. Until we do see something substantive from management, however, I am sticking with my 'hold' rating.

It's too early for Boeing to take flight

Since I wrote my last article about Boeing, the company has only come out with data covering one additional operating quarter. That happens to be the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue was quite strong, totaling $23.95 billion. That represents an increase of 57.1% over the $15.24 billion that the business reported a year earlier. As revenue for the company improved, profitability improved also. The firm went from a net loss of $3.92 billion to a net profit of $8.13 billion. Operating cash flow improved from negative $3.45 billion to $1.33 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from negative $1.62 billion to negative $210 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business increased from $2.52 billion to $4.50 billion.

The chart above shows financial performance for 2025 in its entirety compared to 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, all improved without exception. This is proof that the final quarter of 2025 was not a blip on the radar. Rather, it's part of a consistent uptrend that the company has started to enjoy. To really understand what is happening here, I would say that we need to dig in a bit deeper. And when we do, we can see that the firm is seeing growth across all of its operating segments.

In the image above, you can see financial results for the largest of these, which is none other than its Commercial Airplanes segment. Revenue skyrocketed from $4.76 billion to $11.38 billion for the final quarter on a year-over-year basis. This was thanks to a surge in the number of deliveries from 57 airplanes to 160. Because of issues that the company had years ago associated with a couple of airplanes that had crashed, the firm was constrained in terms of how many airplanes it could produce per month. But as management has proven itself and as backlog for the segment increased, that has started to change.

During the final quarter, for instance, the 737 program increased its production rate to 42 planes per month and received approval from the FAA to start the final phase of 737-10 certification flight testing. A little more than a year earlier, the company was not producing any 737 aircraft. It was only in the final quarter of 2025 that it finally resumed production. The final quarter of the year saw the company start its process of transitioning production under the 787 program to eight aircraft per month. That compares to the five per month that it was producing at the end of the previous fiscal year. Management has made other progress as well. As an example, during the quarter, it began its Type Inspection Authorization 3 phase of its 777-9 certification flight testing as it continues to push for first delivery next year.

The good news is that the company is also bringing in significant orders. This segment booked 336 net orders during the quarter. And it grew its backlog as a result of this to $567.29 billion. That's up materially from the $435.18 billion reported a year earlier. But that's not all of the backlog that the business has. Total backlog increased from $521.34 billion to $682.21 billion over the last year. This was made possible not only because of the increase in demand for its commercial aircraft, but also because of increased demand associated with other parts of the business.

Take, if you will, its Defense, Space & Security segment. In the final quarter of 2025, revenue for this part of the company was $7.42 billion. That compares to the $5.41 billion that the company reported the previous year. According to management, this surge in sales was thanks to higher volumes. This can also be demonstrated when looking at new contract awards, such as the award that it received from the US Air Force for 15 KC-46A tankers, a contract from the US Army for 96 AH-64E Apache helicopters, and the successful delivery of the first operational T-7A Red Hawk to the US Air Force. Thanks to these orders, overall backlog for the segment increased from $64.02 billion to $84.79 billion.

And finally, we have the Global Services segment. Revenue here increased slightly year over year, growing from $5.12 billion to $5.21 billion. Management mentioned that this was thanks to higher government revenue. One thing that is important to note here is that a large surge in the overall net profits for the company involved this particular piece of the business. On October 31st of last year, the company sold off part of that enterprise in a deal valued at $10.55 billion. This cash helped to reduce overall leverage. But the $9.6 billion gain that the company booked on that transaction pushed segment earnings up from just $1 billion to $10.54 billion. Cash flows are more reliable as a result of this than net profits are. But the fact that cash flows are still higher is encouraging. Thanks to a surge in government demand for the services that this part of the company offers, backlog for it expanded from $21.40 billion to $29.72 billion.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year in its entirety, management has provided a bit of guidance. It is highly likely that revenue will rise. And when you add on top of that the benefit of reduced net debt and operating efficiency improvements that the company has been pushing for, it believes that free cash flow will come in at between $1 billion and $3 billion. When you add back to this $4 billion in capital expenditures planned by the company, you are looking at operating cash flow of approximately $6 billion at the midpoint. If we scale up EBITDA similarly year over year, that would give us a reading of about $2.26 billion.

In the chart above, you can see what all of this means for the valuation of the company. Honestly, these are astronomical valuations. And in many cases, a bearish rating would be justified here. But we need to keep in mind the fact that this is a turnaround in progress. It's going to be messy, and it is going to see significant underperformance from a profitability standpoint in the near term. There are, I would argue, better ways to value the company then.

In the chart above, you can see what the price-to-sales and enterprise value-to-sales multiples of the company have been over the last few years now. I'm using the current market capitalization of the company compared to what sales were back then. This means that, right now, the stock actually looks quite attractive. And then in the chart below, you can see something very similar when it comes to both the price-to-backlog and enterprise value-to-backlog multiples. If it weren't for the fact that the business is still experiencing uncertainty associated with its operations, I would actually take this as an opportunity to turn bullish on it. Even as earnings approach, there exists uncertainty.

In the table below, for instance, you can see what analysts anticipate when it comes to the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. They do believe that revenue will rise from $19.50 billion to $22.06 billion. But earnings per share are expected to worsen from negative $0.16 to negative $0.26. This would mean a deterioration on the bottom line from a loss of $123 million to a loss of $209 million. The fact that management is optimistic from a free cash flow perspective for the year indicates to me, however, that cash flows will likely rise, with the worsening in profitability likely the result of certain one time events. I would be surprised if cash flows don't improve year over year.

One other thing that investors should keep an eye on for when management announces results for the upcoming quarter would be net debt. At the end of the most recent quarter, the company had $24.70 billion of net debt on its books. This is actually down from the $30.37 billion that it had in the third quarter, and it compares favorably to the $27.58 billion that the company had at the end of 2024. For context, back in 2022, net debt was an astounding $39.78 billion. Hopefully, management will continue to reduce it as time progresses. With free cash flow now expected to be positive, we have an opportunity for at least some reduction.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I believe that Boeing is on the mend. But I have not seen enough evidence to justify an upgrade for the stock just yet. I do hope that, as the new data comes in, I can become more bullish on it. But out of an abundance of caution, I believe that maintaining it as a 'hold' makes the most sense here.
